data.title: Trustmark Still Has Upside After A Great Run Higher
data.description:
Back in September of 2023, I wrote a bullish article regarding Trustmark Corporation (NASDAQ:TRMK). At the time, the institution was continuing to see deposits grow. The overall financial performance for the business was improving, both on the top and bottom lines. And the overall trend for uninsured deposits was declining. At the end of the day, I argued, shares of the business deserved attractive upside. And since then, the stock is up 83.7%, nearly double the 44.1% achieved by the S&P 500.

Looking at the picture today, I see a financial institution that continues to have high asset quality. Management is continuing to change things around on the balance sheet. Although the stock is just a hair more expensive than I would like it to be, it is certainly affordable. Admittedly, the deposit situation for the firm could be better. But in the grand scheme of things, I think that the overall assessment of the company is still bullish. Because of this, I have decided to keep it as a soft ‘buy’ candidate at this time.

Still banking on Trustmark Corporation

Perhaps the best place to start with when it comes to Trustmark Corporation would be its balance sheet. During the most recent quarter, the financial firm had deposits of $15.12 billion. To be honest with you, this isn't terribly impressive. It was essentially at the same, $15.11 billion, at the end of the 2024 fiscal year. And that represents a decline from the $15.57 billion the company had at the end of 2023. It is worth noting that some of the issues with deposits that the company has faced have been intentional.

In its second quarter earnings press release for the 2025 fiscal year, it said that deposits declined year over year in the second quarter by $347 million because of targeted declines in public funds of $408.2 million, and targeted declines in brokered deposits of $300.5 million. As you will see throughout this article, this is a business that's on a mission to reinvent itself. So cutting out things like brokered deposits, which aren't always reliable and which inevitably have higher interest rates than traditional organic deposits do, is a way to boost overall profitability in the long run.

Even though the value of deposits has dropped, the value of loans on the company's books grew. They increased from $12.93 billion in 2024 to $13.30 billion in the second quarter of this year. The greatest exposure that the firm has when it comes to loans would be non-farm, non-residential properties. They were responsible for $3.48 billion, or 25.8% of the total loans the business has. Secured one to four family residential properties came in second at $3.06 billion. And other sizable amounts of exposure would be other miscellaneous real estate, commercial and industrial loans, and construction, land development, and other land loans. Whenever I see a lot of exposure to non-residential real estate, I do wonder about exposure to office assets. The good news is that management is incredibly transparent here. At the end of the most recent quarter, the firm had $233.5 million of loans accounting for just 1.7% of its total loan portfolio, dedicated to office properties. Most of these are located in Mississippi and Alabama. But the firm does have other exposure such as Texas and Florida as well.

Where management has been making a lot of changes over the last few years now has been the securities portfolio. The firm had $3.08 billion worth in the most recent quarter. That was down from the $3.52 billion the company had at the end of 2022. In fact, in each completed fiscal year, the value of securities has dropped. And increasingly, the firm is shifting from securities that are being held to maturity to those being held for sale. Management has stressed that they continue to focus on strategic goals. This has included the repricing of not only loans, but also the unloading of securities in order to boost net interest margin. Management is also focused on achieving organic loan growth, as well as buying up other players in the industry. But I digress.

Even though securities have dropped, the value of cash and cash equivalents has risen from the end of last year when it was $567.3 million to the second quarter of this year when it totaled $634.4 million. This is still down from the $975.3 million the company had at the end of 2023. But at least we are seeing some improvements here. Simultaneous to that, however, we have seen a bit of downside. Debt has actually risen, hitting $1.23 billion compared to the $840.5 million the company ended last year with.

Despite all of these changes with the balance sheet, Trustmark Corporation has seen some improvements on its top and bottom lines. From 2022 through 2024, net interest income expanded from $471.8 million to $543.2 million. The growth in the value of loans and the decline in the value of debt that it had on its books at the time, not to mention changes in the interest rate environment and the company's approach to it, has allowed its net interest income to rise. Part of this was aided by the growth in net interest margin from 3.17% to 3.51%. As the chart above illustrates, we have seen improvements for the first half of this year compared to the same time last year, and even for the most recent quarter on its own relative to the same time last year. This is great to see.

Non-interest income is more complex. Take the most recent quarter as an example. It totaled $39.9 million. That's up considerably from the negative $141.3 million the business reported one year earlier. As management made changes to its strategy regarding securities, it ended up booking a $182.8 million loss on them in the second quarter of 2025. That explains why the picture here was so bad. Without this, non-interest income would have declined slightly from the $41.5 million it would have been on an adjusted basis. Net income has been significantly affected by these one-time events that have impacted the company. But the good news is that, if the rest of this year looks like the first half of the year did, the company could be on track to generate $219 million in net profits. That would be higher than it has achieved in any of the last three completed fiscal years before this.

Usually when I look at a player in this industry, I like to see it trade at a price to earnings multiple of 10 or lower. As the chart above illustrates, Trustmark Corporation is a hair higher than that at 10.3. That's not a deal breaker. It is worth mentioning that, in that chart, you can see that the company is trading cheaper than four of the five firms that I compared it to. In the chart below, I then looked at the picture through the lens of the price to book and price to tangible book multiples. In both of those cases, the firm was trading just a bit above book value. It's also more expensive they have three of the five firms on a price to book basis and two of the five on a price to tangible book basis, solidifying it right in the middle of the competition. Ideally, I would want the price to book multiple to be at or below 1.

Even though the stock might be just a bit more expensive than I would like it to be, I do appreciate its asset quality. In the first chart below, you can see that it has return on assets of 1.21%. That's above the 1% minimum threshold that I like to see and it places the company higher than three of the five firms that I compared it to. In fact, it's tied with one other player as the second highest on the list. In the subsequent chart below that, you can see its return on equity. I usually like to see a return on equity reading of 10% or higher. And at 10.97%, Trustmark Corporation fits that criterion. It's also above four of the five competitors I compared it to.

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided, I do think that Trustmark Corporation remains an interesting prospect. I definitely think the easy money has been made at this point. Shares are decently priced and management continues to make good progress in key areas. I am especially enthusiastic about the firm's asset quality. Given these factors, I think that keeping it as a soft ‘buy’ rating makes sense.

data.title: Monro: Q1 Progress Makes The Turnaround Story Attractive (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Monro, Inc. (NASDAQ:MNRO) reported the company’s fiscal Q1 results from the April-June period on the 30th of July. The automotive repair shop operator reported positive sales signals amid newly appointed CEO Peter Fitzsimmons’ turnaround efforts, as Monro is stripping the store network and streamlining operations in remaining locations.

I remained at a Hold rating in my previous May article, titled “Monro: Worth Keeping An Eye On”. The stock has since returned 15%, slightly above the S&P 500’s return of 7%. Yet, the stock still values Monro's underlying cash flow very cheaply.

Monro’s Q1 Showed Significantly Improved Sales Momentum

Monro showed good turnaround progress in Q1. The company already previously turned back to positive comparable store sales growth in Q4, and the report showed a continuation of the positive sales trend reversal at an even better level – comparable store sales grew by 5.7%, pushing revenues to a total of $301.0 million and beating Wall Street’s consensus by $4.9 million. The figure is up by 2.7% year-on-year as Monro’s store closures currently cause a headwind on total growth, countering a part of Monro’s comparable sales growth momentum.

As Monro’s long-term earnings plunge has been driven by a very weak comparable sales trend, such comparable store sales momentum is a very positive signal for Monro's turnaround. The metric’s strength was shown in multiple product categories; front & end shocks showed 26% comparable growth, but all other product categories also grew when excluding flat alignment sales. Monro is still planning to improve the customer experience and streamline marketing efforts, which should drive further traffic gains gradually in upcoming quarters. Monro is focusing more on higher-value customers with a simplified assortment.

Negatively, comparable store growth has slowed down throughout Q1 and into July – July only showed 2.0% comparable growth. The July level is still healthy, but makes the next Q2 report critical – further deceleration could be cause for worry. Monro’s sales turnaround is far from certain, even though the Q1 report showed very good strength.

Profitability was still under pressure despite good sales momentum; the gross margin declined by 170 basis points to 35.5% due to continued labor cost inflation. Adjusted operating income ended up at $14.0 million at a 4.7% margin, down by $0.7 million year-on-year. Sales leverage from comparable growth did drive good earnings leverage, offsetting some pressure from labor costs.

Tariffs could cause some further margin pressure in upcoming quarters. Monro is actively working with vendors and distributors to mitigate tariffs. As price increases are still inevitable, the consumer reaction to raised prices is a key question. Ultimately, Monro’s business should be relatively defensive, making consumer demand more inelastic and enabling good pricing power.

Over the longer term, comparable store sales growth remains a critical earnings driver and remains the key focus point in Monro's turnaround. Tariffs’ impact should be mitigated quite well over the long-term, and labor cost inflation should also subside. Monro’s Q1 adjusted EPS of $0.22 beat Wall Street’s consensus by $0.07.

Store Closures Are a Major Positive

Back in May, Monro announced the closing of 145 underperforming stores. The company has already closed down operations in all of the 145 locations by the end of May with very swift execution. Monro continues operations in the remaining 1115 locations.

While store closures don’t signal strength, I believe that Monro’s decision to cut locations is a clear positive – the underperforming stores have clearly been dragging down Monro’s performance. The closed stores only generated approximately 5% of FY2025 revenue while representing 11.5% of Monro’s store footprint; the closed locations generated massively lower revenues than remaining ones. Some revenue losses from store closures are also expected to carry onto nearby Monro locations. Monro only expects the store closures to cause a $45 million revenue headwind in FY2026, just 3.8% of FY2025 revenues. Due to significantly lower per-store revenues, closed locations’ profitability has likely been immensely weak. Peter Fitzsimmons notes the stores to have been unprofitable, and that closures are meaningfully positive to Monro’s profitability.

Further, Monro owns the real estate in 40 of the closed locations, providing significant cash flow through the real estate exit process. Monro expects to largely complete the real estate sale process within the next twelve months.

Monro’s 10% Cash Flow Yield Is Attractive

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Monro. I assume revenue growth to be pressured in FY2026 due to store closures, in part offset by comparable store growth momentum. Afterwards, I have kept my expectations quite moderate, suggesting 1.5% perpetual growth after FY2026 through modest comparable growth.

As Monro’s store closures should improve profitability, I still expect the EBIT margin to expand slightly to 3.5% despite some tariff pressure. Monro’s $69.4 million in depreciation & amortization and significantly lower capital expenditures, $26.4 million in FY2025, continue to make Monro’s cash flow significantly better than GAAP earnings. I expect FY2026 cash flow to be especially good with the inclusion of real estate asset proceeds.

The estimates put Monro’s fair value at $21.69, 54% above the stock price at the time of writing – even with quite modest turnaround progress, the stock has significant upside potential due to depreciation that hides Monro’s underlying cash flow. Even though the turnaround still stands on uncertain ground as comparable store sales growth has decelerated, I believe that the valuation now provides a clearly favorable risk-to-reward. When subtracting the $18.6 million LTM level in interest expenses from my unlevered free cash flow estimates, I assume the stock to currently have a significant 10.2% cash flow yield in FY2027.

The fair value estimate is up slightly from $20.99 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 8.20%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 6.79% interest rate, annualized from Monro’s Q1 interest expenses and debt. I again assume a high 50% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.23% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.13. With a liquidity premium of 0.3%, the cost of equity stands at 9.75% and the WACC at 8.20%.

Takeaway

Monro’s turnaround has showed great progress as comparable store sales have turned to good growth. Operational initiatives should drive further traffic gains, meanwhile recent deceleration in the metric is still a concern if comparable store sales growth continues to slow. Profitability is seeing pressure from labor cost inflation and tariffs, but such pressure should mostly be mitigated; the focus remains on sales improvements. Monro’s unprofitable store closures are also a major positive. With a valuation that provides a significant cash flow yield, and improved comparable growth in remaining stores, I upgrade my rating on Monro to Buy.

data.title: Boot Barn Keeps Riding On The Back Of Western, But ~30x Earnings Is Too Much
data.description:
As so often happens, Boot Barn (NYSE:BOOT) just keeps positively surprising, and adding good quarters on top of good quarters.

The company performed across the line with revenue growth, margin growth, product penetration, opex leverage, everything, in 1Q26 (calendar 2Q25). I cannot find one blemish on the name, except that it is enjoying the tailwind of Western wear.

Alas, it is too expensive for me, as it has come back to 28x earnings. Owning a niche retailer with full-store operations margins at almost 30x earnings in a macro uncertain period is extremely risky, and therefore, I need to let the Boot Barn opportunity go. I maintain my Hold.

A quarter to hang on your wall

Reading the company’s earnings release and call led me to wish I was a Boot Barn shareholder, to be honest. It is rare when one can see a company performing so well for so long along almost every metric, compounding an underlying fashion and cultural trend.

The company posted 9.4% same-store sales growth, which is simply impressive. True, last year was more moderate (1Q25 comps were just +1.2%), but still—very good. Moreover, the momentum seems to be holding, with July comps up +12% according to the call.

Gross margins obviously levered in such a context, especially remembering that lease costs are part of COGS in Boot Barn’s accounting (see 10-Q), meaning that higher store productivity shows up in gross margins more than in operating margin.

Even in the SG&A arena, the company performed, with SG&A flat as a percentage of revenues YoY, despite the company changing management in the past twelve months, facing tariff-related reaccommodations, and expanding the store count by 15% YoY. Management said they have invested more on marketing but cut on compensation. Music to my ears.

On top of that, the company’s underlying western trend is alive and well, as more recently evidenced by the memetic campaign by American Eagle (AEO) featuring Sydney Sweeney in a full denim outfit. The company’s apparel sales are almost 50% denim, according to management.

Tariff headwinds incoming

The call was pretty shallow on tariff specifics, not measuring the specific COGS impact. In fiscal 4Q25 (approximately 1Q25 calendar), Boot had already said that about 10–15% of product would come from China in FY26, so the impact would not be as much, but still, if Vietnam is an important sourcing destination, tariffs could still be relevant.

With merchandise margins of 50%, a tariff of 20% eats 20% of the merchandise margin, and significantly reduces store productivity if unaddressed.

The company’s strategy at the beginning will be to absorb the cost (which again, was not fully disclosed) on its own products (40% of revenues) and pass on the manufacturer-suggested price increases of about mid-single-digits on third-party brands. These are being implemented via re-ticketing across stores and will be completed by August. The company will wait until October (or post-holiday in January) to raise prices on exclusive brands, if at all.

We are yet to see the impact on aggregate sales. The company believes it is well-positioned because tariffs will impact everyone, and because it has larger operations, better inventory capacity, and owned brands. I believe this is all true, but the impact on discretionary income should not be ignored.

In fact, given that the company is guiding for a same-store sales growth level of between -0.5%/3.5% for the year, then we have to assume that 2H26 will be meaningfully slower in terms of comps.

What is there not to like? The price

Again, I wish I were a happy Boot Barn owner, reading such a great earnings release and thinking that a capable management team is working for me. However, I cannot be, because the price (28x the midpoint of guided earnings for FY26) is simply too expensive given the risks: trend and macro.

On the trend, it is clear that Boot Barn leads in western, but the question is how long it will last. It could be years, potentially. However, once that trend recedes, it will probably recede secularly as well, implying years of headwinds for Boot Barn. The name should be evaluated taking this into account.

The second risk is more short-term, for discretionary income and macro impacts in the US (Boot Barn has full exposure to the US market), as people have to pay more for many products and services around them because of tariffs.

The problem with Boot’s price is that it is reflexive (or self-reinforcing) on the two risks above. As the trend improves, the company sells more per store, which in turn makes it more productive and profitable, again feeding the earnings multiple. But if the original factors become headwinds (trend or macro-wise), then store productivity decreases even more (because of operational leverage), and I would believe that the multiple would decrease even more than that.

For that reason, although I like the company’s direction, execution, and envy its shareholders, I cannot buy the name at 28x peak earnings, and therefore maintain a (sad) Hold rating.

data.title: Quantum Computing Is Cool - But IonQ's Valuation Isn't
data.description:
If you didn’t notice, there’s been a party in the market since April, every stock with a buzzword like “quantum” or “robotics” is blowing up, with no regard for valuation. IonQ (NYSE:IONQ) is one of the leaders in this area, offering a pure quantum package, from hardware to cloud-based quantum services on demand, quantum networking, and even the quantum internet. Up to this point… amazing. So sexy. But there’s one problem: we’re not there yet… not even close. With a super-expensive valuation, and dreams hanging on a tree, this stock is an Hold.

Background

First, it’s important to get a better understanding of what quantum is, and where the sector currently stands in a few lines, as I guess most of the readers are not physicists.

If a computer works with bits of 0 or 1, quantum computing is based on qubits — which can exist in a superposition of 0 and 1 at the same time. This allows quantum computers to process multiple possibilities in parallel. Quantum computing does not aim to replace computers, but to solve specific types of problems, that would take traditional computers years... such as quantum simulations for medicine, complex cryptographic algorithms, and optimization tasks in machine learning.

Up to this point.. revolution!!! So… buy quantum stocks?

Not so fast. Quantum computing is still a trial technology with many challenges. First, quantum systems are highly sensitive to the environment from noise to temperature. To create a reliable qubit for actual computation, you often need hundreds of physical qubits plus an error correction system. And as you scale up and add more qubits, the chance of errors increases significantly.

There are several methods to create qubits, each one has its own advantages and disadvantages, And as you'll see , neither approach is exactly the most stable.

In the table below, you can see the main differences between the 3 main methods. There are some important definitions here, like coherence time, which related to how long a qubit can maintain its quantum state before it collapses due to environmental noise. And gate fidelity, related to the accuracy of the qubit. For example, high fidelity means fewer errors during computation.

As you can see, trapped-ion qubits are more accurate and have significantly longer coherence times compared to superconducting qubits, however they are slower and harder to scale. On the other hand, Superconducting qubits are easier to scale and operate faster, but they’re more sensitive to noise and need ultra-cold environments to function.

Regarding Photonic qubits, they are great for communication, they’re fast and resistant to noise, but hard to control precisely, so their gate fidelity is usually lower.

IonQ Story

After we’ve learned some terms, we can dive into the company. So, IonQ is a pure quantum player based on trapped-ion qubits, building its own hardware and providing its flagship quantum computer, Forte Enterprise, which can be used through the popular cloud providers (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud) or as stand alone. This is critical to the company's roadmap, as IonQ expects to build recurring revenue through it.

In addition, IonQ provides quantum network, a technology that aims to allow encrypted communication, it's super safer compared to the internet network, and in my opinion, it is a clear advantage, however its market cap is small.

So, the key client of IonQ who takes the full package is Chattanooga town.

It was the first to use the company’s full quantum system (quantum + network) as part of a smart grid project and for research purposes.

In the Hardware segment, the company bought Oxford Ionics for up to $1.1 billion. Oxford's big breakthrough was replacing complex lasers with chips, making their system easier to scale. They also set a record with 99.9992% gate fidelity.

While Oxford chip looks promising, it has yet to be proven at scale.

Another interesting area in which the company aims to operate is the quantum internet. However, I don’t see any reason to expand on it, as it is still far from us like the quantum satellite that China launched into space. This is a future vision of a more secure internet, but in practice, we are still in the research stages, far from real implementation.

Qubits Roadmap

During the last earning calls, the Chief Executive Officer Niccolo de Masi said:

IonQ now benefits from an accelerated path to tens of thousands of qubits and a clear path to millions of qubits.

The company dreams big, and expects millions of qubits in the future - the more qubits, the more true quantum advantage. However, I don’t want to be the party pooper, but it’s important to note that scaling quantum systems is not just about adding more qubits. It’s about maintaining coherence, fidelity, and error correction at scale - something no company has demonstrated yet."

Is there any proof today that quantum computing is better than classical?

I’ll start with a tip... in this sector, you need to distinguish between the facts and the dream.

The Chairman Peter Chapman said during the last earnings call:

we showcased progress with AstraZeneca and Ansys, where we demonstrated a 12% simulation time improvement using their engineering design software.

In my opinion, 12% improvement is not a game changer that justifies the complex operation. Another fact: there’s been a breakthrough in quantum machine learning, which improves the accuracy of LLM models. The Chief Executive Officer said:

We celebrated National Quantum Day at the New York Stock Exchange last month and unveiled two quantum machine learning breakthroughs from our applications teams for the industrial AI sector. Our own internal research in quantum AI is showing very encouraging signs of improving prediction accuracy in large language models, as well as using quantum data analysis with sports data to highlight performance gaps.

For me it's very amorphous.

Also note that most clients purchase the computer for research purposes only. It is not yet commercial, and there is no monthly subscription, which can imply instability in revenues.

Valuation

The company has a market cap of no less than $12 billion while its revenue in the last quarter was $7.6M with 0% YoY growth.

As a startup-style company, it is losing money and burning cash, with an EPS of -$0.14 last quarter, and an operating loss of $33 million. The company recently raised nearly $1 billion through a secondary equity offering, and has $17m in debt.

For the next quarter, the company expects revenue between $16 and $18 million. As you can see there is no clear trend in revenue.

The company has a price-to-sell that stands at 219, which is huge.

it takes me to the main point of this article.

Main Thesis

If there is something the market hates, it's uncertainty. The more uncertainty we get, the higher the risk, and the volatility rises with it. In the quantum area, there is huge uncertainty. Just look at the wide range in the market cap predictions.

According to McKinsey, in 10 years (2035), the range (in quantum computing + quantum communication) is between $39B to $87B. As you see, it’s super wide, which implies that this business is still unpredictable. For example, according to MarketAndMarkets, in 2029 the market cap for quantum computing is just $5B a 32% CAGR.

So let’s try to calculate the valuation of the company based on this unclear prediction. First of all, I hope you agree with me that a company such as IONQ is considered like a startup-style company in terms of risk, so the average investor expects a huge return from this speculative investment if the business succeeds.

Ultra Bull case - The market cap is $72B... something in the middle-upper range for 2035. Let’s assume a 35% CAGR, that’s around $17B in 2030. And let’s say IONQ is the best of the best and will take 5% of the market share. Don’t forget, if this business really succeeds, all the big players with deep pockets will join the party.

That’s $0.85B in revenue. Let’s give the company a Price-to-Sales of 26, that’s super high, so the market cap is almost $22B, which implies 83% upside in 5 years. I do expect a quantum-style investment to give more upside in 5 years, but we can argue about it.

Bear case - The market cap is $28B in 2035, which is $10B in 2030. This implies $500M in revenue for a 5% market share. With a P/S of 26, and let’s be honest, if the market is this small, I don’t know if it will justify such a high P/S, but OK...that’s a $13B market cap, which implies 8% in 5 years. Not nice.

Ultra Bear case —There will be no real use case that justifies quantum. AI will enhance classical computers. Let’s say the market cap is just $5B in 2030. With 5% market share, that’s $250M in revenue.With a normal P/S of 5, that’s a $1.2B market cap, which implies 90% downside.

Peers

As you may have noticed earlier, there is no specific method that is stable for creating qubits, so there is no single player with a clear advantage.

If we look at the trapped ion approach, IonQ faces competition from Quantinuum, a private subsidiary of Honeywell (HON). According to Quantinuum, its Model H2 system holds a record in Quantum Volume. The problem is that the company is private, and while you can get exposure through Honeywell, quantum is only a small part of Honeywell’s overall business, which gives limited exposure to the quantum segment.

For comparison, I chose the key pure-play quantum companies and added IBM, which I will expand on later.

In terms of market cap, you can see all the companies have high valuations compared to their revenue. The EV/S range is from 150 to 4550 - a bubble, and there’s no other word for it. There is no company showing an impressive growth trend in revenue except QBTS, (QBTS) and only in the last quarter. There’s a lot being one time pay for the quantum computer, and not through recurring revenue or subscriptions, which impacts the ability to create stable and growing revenue streams.

Now let’s talk about cash burn: all the companies are burning cash. As you can see, they are losing money with operational losses every quarter. Their cash positions are shrinking, and all the companies are at risk of needing to raise capital, which would dilute shareholders.

So if you look at the table, you might say: “The best short here is RGTI (RGTI) - small revenue, $5B market cap, big losses, and negative growth.”

I have another perspective.

First of all, in my opinion, all these companies are short - a bubble with no justification for this kind of valuation.

However, I suggest looking at the company with the biggest market cap IonQ, at $12B. The other companies, with smaller market caps, can still surprise. There’s room for their valuations to grow. But the size of the quantum computing market is limited, and IonQ’s valuation already reflects all the future potential, meanwhile, other companies still have the chance to surprise with new developments and grab a larger share of the future market.

Why did I add IBM to the group?

IBM (IBM) also develops quantum computers and could benefit from the revolution. IBM is a stable company. While quantum is a small part of its business, I can promise you that if this wave gains momentum, the business will grow. So you can buy IBM for quantum computing exposure for someone who wants exposure but also wants to manage risk.

Risk in shorting IonQ

It’s important to understand market sentiment. If the sentiment turns into “bubble mode,” these kinds of companies can fly higher and higher. It’s currently worth $12B on just $43M in revenue... so tomorrow it could be worth $20B. There’s no rationality here. That’s what makes it very risky.

Another risk is the unpredictable nature of this business. It’s like developing a new drug...tomorrow we might discover that quantum computing brings a 100% improvement in simulation time, and suddenly everything changes.

Summary

In my opinion, the writing is on the wall. Yes, there’s a chance the quantum bubble will blow up and everything will fly, regardless of fundamentals, but this is a 100% speculative investment.

If you look at the numbers and valuation: IonQ is burning cash and hasn’t proven itself in quantum computing, not in a way that justifies the heavy development costs of the computer.

Add to that the fact that the total market cap estimates for quantum computing aren’t so high compared to IonQ’s current $12B market cap.

data.title: Walt Disney, McDonald's, Palantir Set To Report Earnings
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street opened in the red on Friday, and yields tumbled as the jobs data came in cooler than expected, and traders also assessed President Donald Trump's revised tariff orders. Trump on Thursday signed an executive order revising "reciprocal" tariffs on dozens of countries, setting new duty rates between 10% and 41%. Wall Street on Thursday limped to a negative finish as a post-earnings surge in tech titans was overshadowed by jitters over tariffs and economic data that showed stubborn inflation and weak personal spending.

With Trump's revised tariff orders, investors will be keeping a close eye on economic reports due to be announced next week. These include Factory Orders on Monday, and data on Exports, Imports, and Trade Balance on Tuesday. Wednesday will see the release of MBA Purchase Index and Crude Oil Imports, with Jobless Claims and Consumer Credit are set to be released on Thursday. Friday will see the release of Baker Hughes Rig Count.

Palantir (PLTR), ON Semiconductor (ON), AMD (AMD), Caterpillar (CAT), Pfizer (PFE), Apollo Global Management (APO), Yum! Brands (YUM), McDonald's (MCD), Walt Disney (DIS), Shopify (SHOP), and DoorDash (DASH) will report earnings next week.

_____________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, August 4: Palantir, ON Semiconductor. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, August 5: AMD, Caterpillar, Pfizer, Apollo Global Management, Yum! Brands. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, August 6: McDonald's, Walt Disney, Shopify, DoorDash. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, August 7: Eli Lilly (LLY), ConocoPhillips (COP), Block (XYZ), Take-Two (TTWO). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Opendoor Technologies (OPEN) and New Fortress Energy (NFE) are set up for a volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Celcuity (CELC), Eqv Ventures Acquisition Corp. II (EVAC), and Rich Sparkle (ANPA). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include NextNRG (NXXT), Boss Energy (OTCQX:BQSSF), and Charter Communications (CHTR).

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend next week include Citigroup (C), Blackstone (BX), Lazard (LAZ), Baker Hughes Company (BKR), Metlife (MET), EQT (EQT), Crown Holdings (CCK), Wells Fargo (WFC), and International Business Machines (IBM). Read through some dividend stock picks from Seeking Alpha analysts.

IPO Watch: Firefly Aerospace (FLY) is expected to price its IPO and start trading during the week. The Texas-based space and defense technology company is looking to raise up to $631.8 million by selling 16.2 million shares of common stock at an expected price range of $35 to $39 per share. The analyst quiet periods expire on Ten-League International (TLIH), Delixy Holdings (DLXY), and Almonty Industries (ALM) to free up analysts to post ratings.

McDonald's Earnings Preview: McDonald's is set to report its second quarter earnings next week. Analysts on a consensus expect the fast food chain to report earnings per share of $3.15 on a revenue of $6.7B. Goldman Sachs upgraded McDonald's to a Buy rating last month after having the restaurant stock slotted at Neutral. Analyst Christine Cho and her team said that while there have been some bumps in the road in the last few quarters, the firm believes McDonald's ultimately has the scale/marketing/digital advantage to successfully navigate through the environment.

Palantir Earnings Preview: Palantir will report earnings next week and is expected to report EPS of $0.14 on a revenue of $939.47M. Palantir Technologies' new deal with the U.S. Army worth up to $10B is an additional tailwind for the company, according to Wedbush. The firm maintained its Outperform rating and $160 price target on the shares of Palantir. The analysts expect to hear more about this deal along with strong AI driven results when the company reports earnings on Monday after the bell. The analysts noted that they are expecting robust, AI-driven results next week.

data.title: BMEZ: This Healthcare Fund Is Paying Out More Than It Earns
data.description:
Overview

BlackRock Health Sciences Term Trust (NYSE:BMEZ) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide a diverse exposure to high quality healthcare businesses. As the name implies, BMEZ is a termed fund with a maturity that was set for twelve years, which means that the fund has an estimated liquidation in 2032. The fund has an inception dating back to 2020 and has a management fee of 1.25%. However, the fund puts an emphasis on income generation, which makes it an attractive choice for income investors seeking a high yield.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that BMEZ's share price has declined by about 12.3%. The overall healthcare sector has lagged behind other industries, so this price decline comes as no surprise. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, BMEZ has a total return of only 1.5% over the same period. One of the most attractive aspects to the fund is the starting dividend yield of 14.2% and monthly distributions. However, the recent reporting indicates that the fund's performance has not been sufficient to fully support the distributions that are paid. Therefore, the overall sustainability of the distribution is questionable at this point.

Due to the healthcare sector's ongoing weakness, BMEZ trades at an attractive discount to NAV valuation. For investors that are optimistic about the outlook of healthcare, this could present an attractive opportunity for investors to start accumulating shares. However, there are certainly some risks and vulnerabilities of the fund in the short-term. For instance, the distributions may continue to get reduced if the internal performance doesn't improve. Additionally, the fund includes option writing as a part of the strategy, which can limit the overall total return potential of the fund. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that BMEZ implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, BMEZ has total managed assets of $958M that are spread across 157 different positions. The fund aims to invest at least 80% of its assets into equity securities that operate within the health science sub-sector of healthcare. Although the fund is most heavily focused on companies with primary operations in the US, BMEZ also provides a bit of global exposure as follows:

  • United States: 81.4%
  • Switzerland: 6.7%
  • China: 3.2%
  • United Kingdom: 2.2%
  • Germany: 2.2%
  • Denmark: 1.8%
  • Japan: 1.0%
  • Israel: 0.9%
  • Belgium: 0.7%

As we can see below, the fund is most heavily concentrated on assets that are Pharma, Biotech, & Life Science related, which makes up 59.3% of the fund. This is followed by exposure to Health Care Equipment & Services and Tech Hardware investments, which account for 37.7% and 2.24% of assets respectively. However, investors should expect the top sector to have the greatest impact on BMEZ's daily price movement and total return potential.

When looking at the top holdings, we can see many recognizable large-cap companies. For instance, Alnylam Pharmaceuticals (ALNY) is the largest position at 4.9% of assets. There are also varying weights to other large-cap companies like Veeva Systems (VEEV), Dexcom (DXCM), and Mettler-Toledo International (MTD). BMEZ also provides some private investment exposure with positions like 'Project Picasso'. After some digging through different documents, I was able to locate that Project Picasso is actually related to a company called PsiQuantum.

As a way to generate additional income from its holdings, BMEZ implements an option writing strategy. BMEZ can write options against its own holdings or against the Nasdaq Biotechnology Index. The inclusion of an option strategy means that there's a high probability that BMEZ will underperform many of its own holdings over a longer time horizon. BMEZ can collect option premiums from its positions, which it uses to help fund the distributions to shareholders. The option strategy is essentially trading the growth potential for current income.

Just to demonstrate, I pulled in a price comparison of BMEZ against many of its own holdings. As we can see below, BMEZ's share price has declined by a little more than 30% since its inception. However, many of its own holdings have experienced a high level of growth over the same period, with ALNY appreciating more than 238% over the same time frame.

Valuation

Since BMEZ operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual underlying value of its assets. As a result, BMEZ currently trades at a discount to NAV of about 7%. However, BMEZ has traded at an average discount to NAV of approximately 12.2% over the last three year period, which may indicate that current valuation isn't as attractive at this time. Even looking back over the fund's entire history below, we can see that the current discount to NAV pricing sits at the higher end of the fund's range since inception.

To get better clarity into the fund's internal performance, I reviewed the most recent annual report for 2024. Net investment income amounted to $0.04 per share, while net realized gains were $0.17 per share. Looking back on prior years, the level of net investment income has consistently been negative, indicating a weakness in the fund's ability to generate income. There aren't many holdings within BMEZ that put an emphasis on dividend growth for shareholders, so this poor net investment income performance isn't really a surprise. However, this weakness makes the fund heavily reliant on net realized gains to fuel NAV growth and support distributions.

The only issue is that net realized gains are also very inconsistent and generally reliant on positive market conditions to fuel growth. Option premiums that are collected are typically included as part of net realized gains as well. What's more important is that positive market momentum allows BMEZ to sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. As you can imagine, it can be difficult for a fund to capture positive gains if market indexes are declining and the healthcare sector has trended downward over recent years. This inconsistent performance has resulted in the fund's NAV being significantly reduced since the fund's inception. For reference, here are the fund's year-end NAV amounts since inception:

  • 2020: $30.73 per share
  • 2021: $26.47 per share
  • 2022: $18.76 per share
  • 2023: $17.91 per share
  • 2024: $16.43 per share

The fund's dependence on positive market conditions makes it a very difficult position to recommend for long term income investors. For a fund that puts an emphasis on income generation, I would love to see the fund's net investment income levels improve in the future. Improved net investment income levels would take away from the fund's reliance on positive market conditions and also provide better price and NAV stability for investors. I imagine that an easy way to achieve this would be to include some income securities as a way to boost the level of cash the fund collects.

Risk Profile

One of the most interesting risks for the fund has been the higher interest rate environment. Despite BMEZ not utilizing any leverage, the fund has demonstrated an inverse relationship to the federal funds rate. Shortly after the fund's launch, the federal funds rate was cut to near zero levels as a way to provide stimulus in response to the pandemic. This made it very cost efficient for businesses to borrow capital as a way to fuel operational growth. Healthcare and Life Science companies are generally reliant on access to cheap debt financing as a way to fuel the research and development of new products. The lower interest rate environment caused the fund's share price to rapidly increase throughout 2020 and 2021.

Conversely, BMEZ's share price retracted just as quickly when the federal funds rate was increased to its highest level in a decade. The higher cost of borrowing made it a lot harder for businesses to effectively use leverage as a way to fuel growth. A higher interest rate means that a greater percentage of earnings needed to go towards interest payments on debt. This higher interest rate environment has kept BMEZ's share price suppressed since 2022 and the fund has struggled to regain any positive momentum since then. Therefore, there's a risk that higher interest rates keep the healthcare sector suppressed.

For instance, the Fed has chosen to keep interest rates unchanged in their latest meeting, as they continue to monitor macroeconomic data like inflation, unemployment rates, and consumer spending levels. Additionally, the full impact of President Trump's tariffs are still to be determined, so there's a risk that interest rate cuts will be pushed back until 2026, as I believe the Fed wants to avoid cutting rates too early. Therefore, investors must consider the interest rate risk before initiating a position in BMEZ.

Additionally, the fund's option strategy means that it may not be able to participate in the upside momentum of the healthcare sector. The growth of BMEZ is essentially capped at whatever the selected strike price is. So when the healthcare sector eventually recovers, I believe that BMEZ may get outpaced by more traditional ETFs, such as the Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLV). Just as an example, I pulled in a total return comparison between the two funds and we can see that XLV outpaces BMEZ, despite XLV having a much lower dividend yield of only 1.8%.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1672 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 14.2%. If we multiply this monthly payout by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $2.0064 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, the fund has had trouble generating earnings in excess of this payout amount. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts going back to inception:

  • 2020: $11.63 per share
  • 2021: ($2.55) per share
  • 2022: ($5.97) per share
  • 2023: $0.73 per share
  • 2024: $0.21 per share.

As we can see, BMEZ was only able to generate a sufficient level of earnings back in 2020, which was when interest rates were low. The fact that the fund is now paying out more than it actually earns means that I have concerns about the distribution sustainability going forward. We can see below that the payouts were reduced in 2023 due to weaker earnings. Based on the earnings for 2024, I believe that the fund's distribution policy is too generous and a cut is needed. A lower distribution would help relieve the pressure of the fund needed to generate a high level of earnings and it would better preserve the health of the NAV during these unfavorable market conditions.

It's also worth noting that the fund is heavily reliant on return of capital distributions to pay the distributions. Return of capital distributions are typically used when the fund isn't generating enough income to support payouts. While these distributions can be more tax-efficient, they exacerbate the issue of a declining NAV. Therefore, it becomes difficult to recommend a position in BMEZ for income investors at this time.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that BMEZ does not offer a compelling value proposition for investors at this time. Despite the fund trading at a sizable discount to NAV valuation, the internal performance needs to be substantially improved. Net investment income levels need to be improved and net realized gains have been inconsistent. This poor performance also leads to a questionable dividend sustainability for income investors, as I believe that BMEZ would benefit from a cut. The reliance on return of capital also contributes to the fund's declining NAV over time. For these reasons, I believe that BMEZ is a sell at this time.

data.title: I'm Buying This Market Dip With Leverage
data.description:
In my previous article on the broader markets, I projected that the S&P 500 (SP500) would hit all-time highs around mid-to-late summer. Well, it seems that I was right.

It's been a long time since I last bought stocks, ETFs, or call options on red days, so being able to do so on August 1 was a welcome opportunity, following the July nonfarm payrolls report and Trump's latest update on reciprocal tariffs.

In this article, I discuss why I believe an increase in unemployment in the US is extremely bullish for my bull case on the broader markets. As expected by looking at articles on SA, most analysts have tempered down their optimism after the recent jobs report came in weaker than previously estimated. Not in my case. In fact, I loaded the truck, investing the last of the cash I had remaining.

That said, risks remain, as tariffs are back in the spotlight. However, I believe the optimism we have seen since the April lows is intact, as Trump has pushed back (once again, what a surprise!) the implementation of the reciprocal tariffs, conveniently stating that US customs needed extra time to integrate the new reciprocal rates into the Harmonized Tariff Schedule. Of course, I believe this extra week is to allow a brief window to hash out further exceptions or tailored carve-outs that might otherwise have lapsed under an unmovable August 1 start date.

Overall, I expect this dip to be a good buying opportunity for funds that missed the bull run and avoided buying on green days. Therefore, I believe the dip will be short-lived.

I rate the broader market in the US as a strong buy, reiterating my previous thesis. This time, I don't have any price targets in mind due to the lack of historical data in the chart. That said, high $600s is not an unreasonable target before year-end.

Great News, Unemployment Is Ticking Up

I encourage readers to ask themselves the following question and provide their thoughts in the comments section of this article: For large enterprises in the US, what is the single biggest upside from adopting AI into their daily operations?

I have a very strong conviction in my answer: to rightsize their headcount. This is the polite way to say: to maximize their bottom line by aggressively laying off unnecessary employees. You aren't convinced? Fair enough. Take a look at the following statements made by CEOs of US public companies.

Over the last 15 years or so, we went from 300,000 people to 212,000 people... We just got to keep working that down. - Brian Moynihan, Bank of America.

The best leaders get the most done with the least number of resources required to do the job. - Andy Jassy, Amazon.

Flatter is faster. - Marie Myers, Hewlett Packard Enterprise

We’re going to launch 10 products… The challenge I have for our team is: How do we launch those 10 products without adding head count? - Stéphane Bancel, Moderna

At the margin, we’re asking people to resist head count growth where possible and increase their focus on efficiency. - Jeremy Barnum, JPMorgan Chase

And, my favorite one (for fellow developers and engineers):

We, at Meta as well as the other companies working on this, are going to have an AI that can effectively be sort of a mid-level engineer at your company. Over time we’ll get to the point where a lot of the code in our apps … is actually going to be built by AI engineers instead of people engineers. - Mark Zuckerberg, META

But don't worry, Jensen has the answer to this problem:

You’re not going to lose your job to an AI, but you’re going to lose your job to someone who uses AI. - Jensen Huang, Nvidia

Back in the day, I'm talking about the 2000s, headcount was one of the best metrics to gauge for revenue growth. If you suddenly stopped hiring, it meant that you were in big trouble.

The story of Cisco Systems back in 2001 is a great example. After more than 40 straight quarters of growth, Cisco reported its first negative quarter in Q3 2021. After a few weeks of downplaying the slowdown, the company announced a hiring freeze. Within days of that freeze, Cisco laid off 8,500 employees, wrote off $2.2 billion of excess inventory, and saw its share price collapse from $82 to $13.63 over the next year... Yikes!

I have a very strong conviction that in today's landscape, a decline in headcount is highly welcomed by investors in both the public and private markets. In fact, an increase in headcount is a clear indication that management is unable to leverage AI to their advantage. Of course, there are exceptions for companies that require a physical workforce (nurses, oil rig workers, truck drivers, clinics, etc.), at least, until humanoids become mainstream in the next few decades.

You don't believe me, again? Fair enough.

One of the best examples that comes to my mind is AppLovin. Take a look at what happened to revenue growth back in 2021/2022, when the company ramped up its headcount, vs. what happened in 2024, when they rightsized their organization.

Now that I've made my point, I'd like to be direct. Here is one of the main pillars of my bull case:

A decline in headcount, coupled with an increase in unemployment in the US is extremely bullish for US shareholders.

There are two main reasons.

The first one, well, I already said it above. For most companies (especially those in the technology sector), a decline in headcount could be an indication of cost efficiency due to the adoption of AI agents. This efficiency can lead to an improvement in the bottom line, and in some cases, even in the top line, if the company really knows how to leverage AI to its advantage. This is great news for shareholders and bad news for employees (particularly, entry-level ones).

Second, and this is key, an increase in unemployment is extremely bullish from an interest rate perspective. By looking at the current economic data, the Fed has little to no reason to cut interest rates this year. My prediction is that we will have only one rate cut this year. This is against the consensus, which is leaning for two rate cuts by the January 2026 meeting, as shown in the chart below.

However, in the long term, I do believe that unemployment in the US will pick up. And that's when I predict that interest rates will come down drastically. Maybe not to pandemic levels, but likely down by several percentage points from today's range.

It's difficult to know when this rate cut is going to happen, although I propose a very simple indicator: the growth rate of Palantir's US commercial segment. Why?

This segment is driven by enterprises adopting the AIP platform, in my opinion, the benchmark for building and integrating AI agents into an organization's workflow. Below is a chart representing the revenue growth of Palantir's US commercial segment in the US.

Now, before you go on and build an AI agent to do your work at your day job, I warn you that Palantir's AIP is not available for consumers.

Back to my point, as long as this segment is growing, I can say quite confidently that enterprise adoption of AI in the US is ticking up. Therefore, one can only expect a decline in headcount once the AI agents are mature enough and, therefore, an increase in unemployment, as the US job market will tighten. That increase in unemployment will most likely pressure the FED to cut rates. Both of these effects (rate cuts + cost efficiencies) are positive for shareholders of US equities.

Risks

Looking at the near term, tariffs are once again in the spotlight. I considered including below a very interesting chart by Reuters.

The trend above looks favorable, with significant downward revisions from the previously known tariff rates. To keep things clear, I considered including below the import share and the tariff rate for the top 5 trading partners with the US.

The one tariff that caught the market's attention was the hike in the baseline tariff on Canadian goods from 25% to 35%, effective August 1, 2025. Interestingly, Canada is the only trading partner whose rate has been made effective on August 1. Unsurprisingly (to me), the implementation of these reciprocal tariffs was officially pushed back by one week (now August 7, 2025) in order to give US customs extra time to integrate the new rates into their systems.

Conclusion

I cannot help but wonder if the "final" rates we will see next week, particularly for the top 5 trading partners (except China, as the deadline is August 12), will be lower than what we saw today.

If these rates are the same, there is a good chance for another selloff, which I believe could temper some of the recent optimism. Now, is this selloff enough to take profits and stay uninvested? I argue the answer is no, but readers need to remember that I am a high-risk speculator. My appetite for risk (and returns) is abnormal, to say the least, and therefore not suitable for most investors.

As for the unemployment rate, I am not sweating a drop to see the metric ticking up. In fact, I will start to sweat if I see this key metric going down. I certainly believe that large enterprises need to optimize costs by laying off employees (who do nothing productive most of the day). If unemployment goes up, the FED will be forced to cut down interest rates. Both effects are highly favorable for investors (not so much for entry-level employees and the general public).

Overall, I believe the upside is larger than the downside. That's why I reiterate my strong buy rating.

data.title: Comstock Resources: Production Declines Despite $1 Billion In Capex
data.description:
Comstock Resources (NYSE:CRK) lowered its production guidance for 2025 by approximately 6% while keeping its capex budget unchanged. This results in a projection that it could end up with close to $150 million in full-year cash burn now.

Comstock still is expected to have plenty of liquidity, but I've reduced its estimated value by $1 to approximately $13.50 per share now in a long-term (after 2026) $3.75 NYMEX natural gas environment.

This is due to Comstock's weaker production results and the impact of lower 2025 and 2026 natural gas strip prices on its projected near-term free cash flow.

I thought Comstock was very overvalued before, but I believe it is only moderately overvalued (for a long-term $3.75 NYMEX natural gas scenario) now that its shares are down close to 50% from their highs.

Q2 2025 Results

Comstock averaged 1,233 MMCFE per day in Q2 2025 production, which was towards the low-end of its guidance for 1,225 to 1,325 MMCFE per day in average production during the quarter. Comstock's capex was within expectations for the quarter, while its per unit operating costs were near the low-end of its guidance.

Weaker realized prices (as well as slightly higher capex) during Q2 2025 (compared to Q1 2025) contributed to a $79 million free cash deficit from operations during the quarter. This was around zero for Q1 2025.

Guidance Revision

Comstock made a noticeable negative revision to its guidance for 2025. It now expects 1,225 to 1,325 MMCFE per day in average production during the year, which (at guidance midpoint) is a 6% reduction from its prior guidance. Comstock's D&C capex budget was unchanged, while it added $10 million to its acreage budget for the year.

Thus we may have a situation (at guidance midpoint) where Comstock sees a 12% year-over-year decline in production despite spending around $1 billion on D&C capex.

Year 2022 2023 2024 2025E
Production (MMCFE per Day) 1,373 1,438 1,442 1,275
D&C Capex ($ Million) $1,032 $1,272 $902 $1,050

Comstock continues to de-risk its Western Haynesville acreage, but has added back a legacy Haynesville rig in an attempt to reverse some of those production declines.

While it has made significant progress with de-risking the Western Haynesville and becoming more capital efficient there, its legacy Haynesville acreage still appears to offer better returns at this stage. As well, there have been some midstream issues as it builds out its Western Haynesville infrastructure, and that may slightly delay some wells being turned online.

2H 2025 Outlook

Comstock may average around 1,294 MMCFE per day during the second half of the year, based on its revised guidance midpoint. This would be a 3% increase from its 1H 2025 average production.

The NYMEX natural gas strip for 2H 2025 is now around $3.40, and Comstock is projected to generate $763 million in 2H 2025 revenues after hedges.

Comstock is running eight rigs during 2H 2025, including four on its legacy Haynesville acreage and four in the Western Haynesville. This is up one rig on its legacy Haynesville acreage.

Despite the increase, Comstock expects roughly flat capex compared to 1H 2025 as it also is planning for slightly less completion activity during the second half of the year.
