Comstock is thus projected to have around $67 million in cash burn during the second half of 2025.

Notes On Debt

That additional cash burn would increase Comstock's debt up to around $3.1 billion. Comstock still has plenty of credit facility borrowing capacity remaining though and should also be able to keep its leverage ratio below 3.5x unless NYMEX natural gas prices averaged in the mid-$2 range or lower.

Current 2026 strip prices are around $4, which should allow Comstock to generate a modest amount of positive free cash flow next year and potentially lower its leverage to around 2.2x to 2.3x by the end of 2026 instead.

Comstock is exploring non-core asset sales to help it deleverage as well.

Notes On Valuation

I've reduced my estimate of Comstock's value from $14.50 per share to $13.50 per share at long-term (after 2026) $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

This is partly due to weaker near-term strip prices that have reduced Comstock's projected 2026 free cash flow by close to $100 million since I last looked at it.

As well, Comstock's downward production guidance revision also negatively affects its value despite its progress in de-risking the Western Haynesville.

I thought Comstock was greatly overvalued before, but it seems to be getting to around a more reasonable level now. At long-term $4.00 NYMEX natural gas instead, I'd value it at around $17.50 per share, although that's a bit more optimistic than my typical view.

Conclusion

Comstock is now expected to have close to $150 million in 2025 cash burn due to weaker near-term natural gas prices and a downward revision in its production guidance (resulting in expectations for a double-digit decline in year-over-year production).

Comstock continues to make progress with de-risking the Western Haynesville, although its legacy Haynesville assets are still more capital efficient. With its cash burn and weaker production expectations, I have reduced its estimated value to $13.50 per share in my base case scenario for long-term $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

data.title: More Yield For A Little More Risk: Yields Up To 15%
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

When evaluating a sector that has experienced an uptick in volatility, the question becomes, what path should I approach it from to have the best potential outcome for my investment perspective and philosophy?

The MBS (Mortgage-Backed Security) sector is considered to be an extremely low-risk sector because of the government guarantee, especially on agency MBS. This means that companies that invest in this type of asset can utilize a large amount of leverage when secured by the MBS, as the MBS itself is considered extremely low-risk. Various options are available in the market to gain exposure to MBS if desired.

We've previously highlighted an ETF that invests directly in MBS with no leverage and no frills and yields about 6%. It is a great option for an investor looking for a low-risk route, but what if you want to get a little bit more risk from the sector?

Today, I want to look at two ways you can boost your yield, but also accept a little bit more risk in the process. The first option will give you over double the yield of the ETF. The second is only a couple of percentage points of yield.

Let's dive in!

Pick #1: AGNC – Yield 15.3%

AGNC Investment Corporation (AGNC) is a mortgage REIT that invests primarily in MBS. AGNC mixes in leverage to boost returns and income generated from what is overwhelmingly considered an ultra-low-risk investment type. AGNC saw its tangible book value dip to $7.81/share, down about 5.4% from Q1:

However, in their earnings call in Q1, management noted that book value was 7.5%-8% lower as of April 21st, implying a book value of $7.59-$7.63. The driver of book value is influenced both by the price of agency MBS and by the relationship between MBS prices and hedging prices. AGNC hedges by using interest rate swaps and shorting US Treasuries. These are natural hedges because MBS and Treasury prices have a fairly strong correlation. However, a strong correlation does not mean they move in lockstep.

Here is the current coupon MBS spread to US Treasuries and to Interest Rate Swaps: Source

The spread to Treasuries increased only 3 bps. The spread to swaps increased 11 bps. Every quarter, AGNC reports its sensitivity to "basis risk", which is the change in the relationship between MBS and hedges.

Note that a 10 bps increase in spread is anticipated to cause a 5.2% decline in NAV. It was the same in Q1; we saw an 11 bps increase in spread to swaps, which is their primary hedge position right now, and NAV declined 5.3%.

What does this mean in practical terms? Well, as is the case for all financial transactions, lower prices are good for buyers. The spread between MBS and hedging costs is literally how AGNC profits. They buy MBS, they buy hedges, and they profit from the difference. The higher the spread, the higher the profits are for any new positions implemented today. That means older positions bought at lower spreads are less valuable, because you can get a higher return from new purchases.

If we go back to a time when AGNC was trading for $20, like Q4 2017, this spread was 60-70 bps. It's an irony of bonds that the worse the forward return, the higher the prices. The better the forward return, the lower the prices.

AGNC is stepping up as a buyer, adding an additional $3 billion in assets in Q2:

It was able to do this while keeping leverage relatively flat at 7.6x (vs. 7.5x in Q1) by issuing common equity. AGNC's share price traded at a substantial premium to NAV throughout the quarter, which allows it to issue equity freely. In recent years, there have been many investors who have relied on book value as the primary determinant of when to buy or sell agency mREITs. And over the past decade or so, this has been an effective strategy. However, it is noteworthy that during the periods where agency mREITs have outperformed the most, like during the GFC, they traded at very large premiums, and someone looking at book value would have sat on the sidelines.

This quarter, AGNC reported that its book value is well below the trading price, and the market apparently doesn't care—choosing instead to focus on the high cash flow and the forward ROE (return on equity) being about 19%, which is a level we usually see during periods of market panic. It is very rare to have such a high ROE while also having an environment where AGNC can issue equity and buy billions as fast as they can, because usually these high prices coincide with destabilization in the financial markets.

Turning to cash flow, we saw a slight dip in earnings to $0.38/share.

We noted this was likely to happen at last quarter's earnings writing:

"The bottom line is that AGNC's $0.44 in earnings amply covers its $0.36 dividend. We might see a little bit of a dip in earnings based on the timing of interest rate swap maturities and how quickly AGNC can keep increasing its asset yield."

AGNC has swaps that they took out in 2020-2021 that are maturing, and this is gradually increasing their cost of funds. So we expect variation quarter to quarter with earnings, due to the lumpiness of those maturities. We continue to believe that Q4 2024 represents a bottom and that earnings will generally trend upward; it just won't be in a straight line.

We expect AGNC to continue issuing equity as long as they are trading at a premium and will continue to make its portfolio larger. In the earnings call, management was cognizant of the fact that its growth had a practical limit. They want to make sure that their scale doesn't become so large that they have problems transacting in the hedging markets at the volumes they need. However, there is ample room for growth, and the longer that interest rates remain in this gray zone where they aren't really going up, but nobody really knows when they will come down, AGNC has more time to buy higher coupons. That will be a significant positive when the day comes that the equity market falls and the "flight to safety" takes place.

Pick #2 ARR-C – Yield 8%

ARMOUR Residential REIT (ARR) is a mortgage REIT that invests primarily in MBS as well as Treasury notes. ARR is more readily considered a peer to Dynex Capital (DX) than much larger mREITs like AGNC or Annaly (NLY). As such, management's ability to navigate different sector changes greatly impacts its ability to provide strong returns. When we compare ARR common equity returns to those of DX, for example, we can see that the management's ability to navigate and the choices they make have vastly different outcomes:

Historically, ARR has been willing to issue shares when they're trading at a premium and buy back shares when they are trading at a discount to book value. This can make the number of outstanding shares confusing because in the same quarter, they may buy back and sell shares. It also means that ARR's management team is more willing than other management teams to cut or raise the dividend as they feel necessary. This makes, as far as our personal perspective, ARR's common equity unattractive.

If we climb one step higher in the capital stack, we can find a persistently attractive opportunity in the preferred shares that are outstanding.

ARMOUR Residential REIT, 7.00% Series C Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Stock (ARR.PR.C) offers a strong 8% yielding monthly dividend-paying preferred security. ARR-C is currently trading post-call but is highly unlikely to be called in the current interest rate environment. ARR-C's dividend was covered 22x by its distributable earnings this past quarter, which is consistent with the prior year's coverage as well.

ARR-C is a beneficiary of management's ability to continue to sell new common shares to the market, even if those common shares are not providing strong total returns to their shareholders. The coverage of the preferred dividend remains strong and consistently strong, only taking about $3 million per quarter to payout, while ARR is generating in excess of $67 million of distributable earnings.

If ARR-C is unexpectedly called, you would benefit from its current discount to PAR. This, combined with a stable and attractive monthly-paid yield, makes ARR-C a highly attractive, lower-risk avenue to collect strong income from MBS exposure.

Conclusion

By accepting just a little bit more risk, an investor can collect upwards of 16% or 8% yields versus the available 6% yield on traditional MBS at this time. By using ARR-C to collect 8%, an investor can unlock more income without taking on any increased level of significant risk. If an investor wants 16% yields that are paid out monthly from AGNC, they can readily collect that and still gain valuable exposure to the MBS sector. It is a sector that is getting ready to heat up and provide even better total returns, regardless of the avenue in which you decide to have exposure.

When it comes to retirement, matching the yield you'll need for your financial future is important. If you are a well-to-do investor who does not want to collect high levels of income because you're more risk-averse, then ARR-C may be perfect for you. A simple 8% yield without a lot of risk and not a lot of frills. However, if you're an investor who wants more income per dollar invested, AGNC can provide you with a wonderful route to collect high-yield income on a regular basis.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Alphabet: Why An Antitrust Breakup Is Good
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOG) defeat in antitrust court and perceived threat from LLMs to its search engine ad revenue fuel perceptions of an existential crisis. Our view is that, if anything, the rise of LLMs is positive to all of Alphabet's business lines, including search ad revenue. Regarding the DOJ case, all outcomes are positive at this point.

Valuation

Among the magnificent seven, Alphabet has the lowest valuation ratios across all metrics, on both the TTM and Forward basis, except for EV/Sales, where it's second to Amazon (AMZN), only because of the retail giant's razor-thin margins, which justifiably limit what investors are willing to pay per unit of sales.

GOOG might appear overvalued when compared to the Communications sector (its official GICS industry categorization since a 2018 overhaul), as mirrored in Seeking Alpha's Quant Score.

However, when compared to the broader Information Technology market, GOOG is undervalued despite its tech moat, leadership, and brand, as shown in the table below.

Perhaps more telling is that GOOG now trades at a discount to its own historical valuation. For example, GOOG's current PE ratio is about 21% lower than its 5-year average.

Antitrust Issues: Bad News is Good News

Alphabet lost a critical antitrust case in August 2024, and the ramifications could be more serious than anything we've witnessed before.

Until now, the US and EU prosecutors have been content with fines and cease and desist orders, and voluntary changes to business practices.

However, in May 2025, in relation to the August 2024 case, the DOJ proposed breaking up the company, particularly spinning off Android OS, Chrome Browser, Google Ad Exchange "AdX", among other business lines. A Judge is set to determine the appropriate remedies/sentence this month. Investors should brace for some volatility.

Our view is that a "negative" court outcome, i.e, a splitting of Alphabet into smaller companies, is a positive outcome to shareholders, as breaking down the company will unlock value to shareholders, who will receive shares in these said companies. The higher multiples assigned to these newly formed companies will more than offset the negative impact of the loss of synergies on net income.

Take, for example, AppLovin (APP), whose business model aligns with Google AdMob, trades at a forward PE ratio of 48x, about twice that of Alphabet's consolidated PE. Similarly, The Trade Desk (TTD), which competes with Google DV 360 service, has a forward PE of 48x, highlighting the weight that Alphabet's legal issues are putting on its valuation. Magnite (MGNI), which runs the largest independent Ad exchange, also has a forward PE ratio about 36% above that of Alphabet's consolidated PE ratio.

Waymoo, as the #1 autonomous driving technology provider, could also achieve high valuation as a standalone company, despite being unprofitable.

Another example is Snowflake (SNOW), which is smaller and growing at a slower rate than Google Cloud, yet has a forward PE of 200x.

Gemini and YouTube are growing at spectacular rates, and would also attract investors' enthusiasm as standalone companies, unlocking value if Alphabet is broken down.

Alphabet's Tensor Processing Units "TPUs" which are currently used in Alphabet's data centers, could also be turned into a standalone business. Based on the current valuations of fabless semiconductor companies, Google TPU could attract investors' attention, translating to meaningful capital gains to Alphabet's shareholders if it were spun off.

One can argue that the harsher the judge's upcoming sentencing, the better the outcome for investors. Sure, there will be some synergies lost, resulting in slightly lower net income, but this will be offset by higher valuation multiples, in my view.

LLMs Threat Overrated

There are concerns that the rise of generative AI and LLMs will negatively impact Google Search's Ads business. However, Q2'25 results don't seem to support this premise. Google Search revenue rose by 11.7%, in line with the historical average, notwithstanding the COVID pandemic disruptions.

The launch of Search Generative Experience "SGE" resulted in more frequent and deeper interactions with Google Search, according to Philipp Schindler, Alphabet's CBO. This also offsets the lost profiling opportunity from informational queries shifting to other LLMs.

Overall, looking at revenue by segment, I think that generative AI has a positive impact on Alphabet's businesses.

Q2 2024 Q2 2025 YoY Change
Google Search & other 48,509 54,190 12%
YouTube ads 8,663 9,796 13%
Google Network 7,444 7,354 -1%
Google advertising 64,616 71,340 10%
Google subscriptions, platforms, and devices 9,312 11,203 20%
Google Services total 73,928 82,543 12%
Google Cloud 10,347 13,624 32%
Other Bets 365 373 2%
Total revenues 84,742 96,428 14%

Dividends and Capital Allocation

Another concern facing Alphabet is its CapEx spending. During the Q2 2025 earnings call, the company noted that it is raising its CapEx this year to $85 billion, up $10 from the previous guidance, which represents almost a 60% increase from last year's budget.

In my view, the company didn't do itself any favors when it started a dividend program last year, given the CapEx requirement as the company navigates the emerging AI/ML landscape.

The good news is that the company's cash spending and capital returns are almost fully covered by operating cash flows.

Here's the company's capital allocation in the TTM ended June 2025

TTM June 2025 (billion $)
Operating Cash Flow $133.7
CapEx $66.9
Share Buybacks $59.5
Dividends $9.9
Retained Cash -$2.6

Risks

I think that the primary risk facing Alphabet is that fears over its market position become a self-fulfilling prophecy by impacting its ability to attract and maintain talent. Meta's (META) recruiting spree demonstrates the importance of talent in maintaining technological and, by extension, market position. If job-seekers perceive Alphabet's future or technological position as uncertain, they might opt for other companies with clearer career paths, creating a vicious cycle of poor recruiting apparatus, which, in turn undermines market position, further determining talent recruitment.

Summary

Alphabet's ability to grow its revenue and profits despite the changing competitive and technological landscape highlights its agility and solid portfolio positioning in the AI/ML era.

This is not the first time that the company has experienced technological and regulatory change. The present panic reminds me of Alphabet in the mid-2000s, when social media companies started introducing targeted ad services that leveraged users' data, yet Google Search persevered and prospered. Generative AI and LLMs are accelerating digitalization trends, the same tailwinds that helped the company deliver extraordinary results for the past two decades.

Finally, any outcome from the antitrust court judge is a positive one, in my view. Alphabet has demonstrated its ability to navigate cease and desist orders by adjusting its business practices while maintaining growth. If the worst happens, and the company is ordered to spin-off some of its subsidiaries, current investors are in for some lucrative share dividends in the new entities. Finally, the court sentence/remedy will remove the uncertainty that has been weighing on the company's valuation.
